learning_objectives: Map the major regulatory bodies and their jurisdictions in healthcare
Understand how regulations shape health IT design and operations
Differentiate between federal, state, and industry-specific regulations
readings: Health Information Management: Principles and Organization Ch. 12

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare is one of the most heavily regulated industries. Every informatics system operates within a complex web of federal, state, and industry regulations that the informatics professional must navigate.
key_concepts: Key regulatory bodies: HHS (CMS, ONC, OCR, FDA), FTC, state regulators
HIPAA: Privacy Rule, Security Rule, Breach Notification Rule, Enforcement Rule
21st Century Cures Act: information blocking, interoperability requirements
State-specific regulations: consumer privacy laws (CCPA/CPRA), state breach notification
Industry standards as quasi-regulation: Joint Commission, HITRUST, NIST CSF
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map the regulatory requirements for a health IT system: which regulations apply, who enforces them, and what the penalties are for non-compliance

review.value: Office for Civil Rights within HHS: enforces HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules, investigates complaints, and issues civil monetary penalties
21st Century Cures Act prohibits practices that unreasonably prevent access, exchange, or use of electronic health information - enforced by ONC with penalties up to $1M per violation

learning_objectives: Design a comprehensive HIPAA compliance program
Understand the seven elements of an effective compliance program
Develop policies and procedures for HIPAA requirements
readings: HHS OIG Seven Elements of an Effective Compliance Program

lecture_notes.introduction: A compliance program is not just a set of policies in a binder. It is an active, monitored, enforced system that protects the organization and its patients from regulatory violations.
key_concepts: Seven elements: standards/policies, compliance officer, training, reporting mechanism, enforcement, auditing, corrective action
HIPAA Privacy Officer and Security Officer roles
Policy development: Notice of Privacy Practices, access controls, incident response
Workforce training programs: initial, annual, role-specific
Compliance monitoring: internal audits, risk assessments, corrective action tracking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Review a sample HIPAA compliance program structure and evaluate it against the seven elements, identifying gaps

review.value: OIG framework: written standards, designated compliance officer, effective training, accessible reporting channels, consistent enforcement, regular auditing, prompt corrective action
Designated individual responsible for developing and implementing privacy policies and procedures as required by the HIPAA Privacy Rule

class_schedule.topic: Health IT Certification and ONC Requirements
learning_objectives: Understand the ONC Health IT Certification Program
Analyze certification criteria and their impact on EHR design
Evaluate the Conditions and Maintenance of Certification requirements
readings: ONC Health IT Certification Program Overview
21st Century Cures Act Final Rule (ONC) Summary

lecture_notes.introduction: ONC certification determines what EHR systems can do and how they must behave. Understanding certification requirements is essential for anyone involved in health IT development, procurement, or implementation.
key_concepts: ONC-ACB certification process: testing, certification, surveillance
Certification criteria: clinical, interoperability, privacy/security
Conditions of Certification: real-world testing, attestation, FHIR API requirements
Maintenance of Certification: ongoing compliance obligations for developers
Impact of certification on EHR market competition and innovation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Review the certification criteria checklist for a key EHR module (e.g., CPOE or patient portal) and map to product features
setup_requirements: CHPL (Certified Health IT Product List) access

review.value: ONC-Authorized Certification Body: third-party organizations authorized to test and certify health IT products against ONC criteria
Certified Health IT Product List: public database of all ONC-certified health IT products, their certification status, and compliance history

class_schedule.topic: FDA Regulation of Health IT and AI
learning_objectives: Understand when health IT qualifies as a medical device subject to FDA regulation
Analyze the FDA's framework for AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device
Evaluate the regulatory pathway for digital health products
readings: FDA Digital Health Center of Excellence Resources
FDA Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Software as a Medical Device Action Plan

lecture_notes.introduction: As health IT becomes more clinically impactful, FDA regulation becomes more relevant. AI-powered diagnostic tools, clinical decision support, and digital therapeutics all have regulatory implications.
key_concepts: FDA jurisdiction over SaMD: when software qualifies as a medical device
Risk classification: Class I (low), Class II (moderate), Class III (high)
Regulatory pathways: 510(k), De Novo, PMA
Predetermined change control plan for adaptive AI/ML models
Digital health enforcement discretion: what FDA chooses NOT to regulate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Classify three health IT products by FDA risk level and determine the appropriate regulatory pathway for each

review.value: FDA framework allowing AI/ML device manufacturers to pre-specify types of model changes that can be made without requiring new clearance
FDA clearance pathway for moderate-risk devices demonstrating substantial equivalence to a legally marketed predicate device

learning_objectives: Design internal audit programs for healthcare IT compliance
Develop corrective action plans for identified deficiencies

lecture_notes.introduction: Compliance auditing verifies that policies are actually being followed. Without regular auditing, compliance programs become paper exercises that fail to protect the organization.
key_concepts: Internal audit planning: scope, frequency, sampling methodology
HIPAA audit protocol: OCR's audit areas and evaluation criteria
HITRUST CSF: comprehensive framework incorporating HIPAA, NIST, PCI, and other standards
Risk-based audit prioritization: focusing on highest-risk areas
Corrective action planning: root cause analysis, timeline, responsible party, verification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a simulated HIPAA audit of access control practices using an audit checklist and sample system configuration

review.value: Health Information Trust Alliance Common Security Framework: comprehensive certifiable framework harmonizing HIPAA, NIST, ISO, PCI, and other standards
Documented response to audit findings specifying the deficiency, root cause, remediation steps, responsible person, timeline, and verification method

class_schedule.topic: Emerging Regulatory Challenges: AI Ethics, Consumer Privacy, and Global Regulations
learning_objectives: Analyze emerging regulatory frameworks for healthcare AI
Understand state consumer privacy laws and their healthcare implications
Compare U.S. and international health data regulations
readings: EU AI Act Healthcare Provisions Summary
State Consumer Privacy Law Comparison Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: The regulatory landscape is evolving rapidly. AI regulation, expanding state privacy laws, and international frameworks create new compliance challenges for health informatics professionals.
key_concepts: EU AI Act: risk-based classification of AI systems, healthcare as high-risk domain
State privacy laws: Washington My Health My Data Act, state-level health data protections
GDPR and international health data regulations
AI transparency and explainability requirements in healthcare
Cross-border health data transfer requirements and frameworks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compliance gap analysis for a health AI product: mapping requirements across FDA, EU AI Act, HIPAA, and state privacy laws

review.value: Classifies medical AI as high-risk requiring conformity assessment, human oversight, transparency, and post-market monitoring
Emerging state laws (e.g., Washington MHMD Act) creating stricter health data protections than HIPAA, including consent requirements for health data collection by non-HIPAA entities

questions.question: List the seven elements of an effective compliance program (per OIG guidance) and explain how each specifically applies to a health IT organization managing EHR systems.
questions.answer: Seven elements applied to health IT: (1) Written standards: HIPAA policies, information security policies, acceptable use policies for EHR access. (2) Compliance officer: designated HIPAA Privacy and Security Officers with authority and resources. (3) Training: role-specific training on PHI handling for developers, support staff, and administrators. (4) Reporting mechanism: anonymous hotline for reporting potential HIPAA violations or security incidents. (5) Consistent enforcement: disciplinary actions for policy violations (unauthorized access, sharing credentials). (6) Regular auditing: periodic HIPAA security risk assessments, access log reviews, configuration audits. (7) Corrective action: structured remediation for identified deficiencies with tracking and verification.

rubric.key: Health IT-specific application for each element

questions.question: Under the 21st Century Cures Act, what constitutes 'information blocking' and what are the recognized exceptions? Provide an example of a practice that would be considered information blocking by a health IT developer.
questions.answer: Information blocking is a practice by a health IT developer, health care provider, or health information exchange that is likely to interfere with, prevent, or materially discourage access, exchange, or use of electronic health information. Recognized exceptions: preventing harm to patients, protecting privacy, ensuring security, infeasibility, health IT performance, licensing/fees with reasonable terms, maintaining and improving health IT. Example of information blocking by a developer: an EHR vendor restricting FHIR API access to only their own approved apps, preventing third-party SMART on FHIR applications from accessing patient data, even when the patient or provider has requested the data. Penalties: health IT developers face civil monetary penalties up to $1M per violation, enforced by ONC.

rubric.key: Valid developer-specific example with penalty knowledge

questions.question: A company develops an AI tool that analyzes chest X-rays and provides a probability score for pneumonia to assist radiologists. Determine: (a) whether this qualifies as a medical device, (b) its likely FDA risk classification, and (c) the appropriate regulatory pathway.
questions.answer: (a) Yes, this qualifies as Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) because it is intended to provide diagnostic information from medical images for clinical decision-making. (b) Likely Class II (moderate risk) because it provides decision support to a trained radiologist who makes the final diagnostic determination, rather than making autonomous diagnostic decisions. If it were fully autonomous (no physician review), it could be Class III. (c) The appropriate pathway is 510(k) if a substantially equivalent predicate device exists (several AI radiology tools have been cleared), or De Novo if no predicate exists. The company would need to demonstrate: clinical validation (sensitivity/specificity on representative test sets), software documentation (design controls, cybersecurity), and labeling indicating intended use as an assistive tool.

questions.question: Describe the purpose and structure of a HIPAA security risk assessment. What are the consequences of failing to perform one?
questions.answer: Purpose: The HIPAA Security Rule (45 CFR 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(A)) requires covered entities to conduct an accurate and thorough assessment of potential risks and vulnerabilities to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of ePHI. This is a required (not addressable) specification. Structure: (1) Identify ePHI locations and systems, (2) Identify threats (technical, human, environmental), (3) Identify vulnerabilities in current safeguards, (4) Assess likelihood and impact of each threat-vulnerability pair, (5) Assign risk levels, (6) Document findings and prioritize remediation. Consequences of failure: Risk assessment failure is the most commonly cited HIPAA violation in OCR enforcement actions. OCR settlement examples: Premera Blue Cross ($6.85M, 2020), Banner Health ($1.25M, 2023). OCR considers failure to conduct a risk assessment as the foundational compliance failure because all other security decisions depend on it.

major_assignments.title: Healthcare Regulatory Compliance Assessment
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive compliance assessment for a simulated health IT organization, covering HIPAA, 21st Century Cures Act, and FDA requirements. Identify gaps and develop a remediation roadmap.
objectives: Apply regulatory requirements to a realistic health IT scenario
Conduct systematic compliance gap analysis across multiple regulations
Communicate compliance risks and recommendations to leadership
deliverables: Prioritized remediation roadmap with timeline and resource estimates
Executive summary (2 pages) for board-level communication

criteria: All relevant regulations identified and assessed
Gaps clearly documented with regulatory citations
Quick wins and long-term initiatives balanced
Actionable remediation roadmap with realistic timelines

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of regulatory knowledge and compliance concepts

case_studies.description: Analysis of real-world compliance cases and enforcement actions

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of HIPAA, Cures Act, and ONC requirements

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including FDA regulation, auditing, and emerging regulations

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Case study discussions require active participation. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 10% per day. Compliance assessment milestones must be met for iterative feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: Case study discussions are collaborative. All written deliverables must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Regulatory analysis must cite primary sources (CFR, Federal Register, guidance documents). Secondary sources must be verified against primary text.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with internet access for regulatory database research. Access to eCFR, Federal Register, and FDA databases.
